message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T93 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Branch: implement/T93    Base commit: 356d850 (current main HEAD). If the harness cut your worktree from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard 356d850` first (sanctioned base-refresh); confirm internal/bind/multipath.go has the T83-T91 multi-peer demux (peerState, peerBySource, TearDownPeer) and internal/config has PeerIdentities/effective-PSK helpers before starting.
headline: Wire per-peer prober sets, schedulers, and virtual endpoints in device.Up
description: In internal/device, extend buildScheduler/Up so the concentrator builds per-peer wiring: one prober set + scheduler + stable virtual endpoint per configured peer (each peer's probers/reflector keyed on that peer's effective PSK from the config helper), and programs the Bind's receive demux from authenticated peer bindings. Make bind.ProberFactory per-peer (R72): today the factory returned by buildScheduler (internal/device/device.go:577) closes over the single cfg.PSK; replace it so a prober created for a (peer,path) pair keys on THAT peer's effective PSK, and so a RUNTIME-added path gains a prober per bound peer (and a removed path tears each bound peer's per-(peer,path) prober down) — the runtime path add/remove flow must work while >=2 peers are bound. Report each peer's stable virt endpoint to the engine (A1); map WG peer public keys to bind peer identities so uapiConfig (device.go:706) and the Bind agree on the peer set. Keep the single-peer path structurally identical to today. Keep startHubFailover edge-only and unchanged.
acceptance: `go build ./...`; a device-level test brings up a 2-peer concentrator config yielding two peerStates each with its own prober set/scheduler/virt endpoint; a single-peer concentrator config produces exactly one peerState and unchanged wiring; uapiConfig golden output for existing single-peer fixtures is byte-identical. A runtime-path test adds a path while 2 peers are bound and asserts each bound peer gains a prober keyed on its OWN PSK for the new path, and path removal tears down each peer's per-(peer,path) prober; the existing runtime-path suites (internal/bind/runtime_path_test.go, tolerant_membership_test.go) still pass. `go test ./internal/device/...` passes.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'` — go not on bare PATH): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. The single-peer golden byte-identity is a CRITICAL no-regression guard — verify it. Mutation-verify the per-peer PSK keying (a prober keyed on the WRONG peer's PSK must fail a test).
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t93-fix` off 356d850 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha (copy from git output). Sync docs (docs/design.md) if you change observable architecture. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

676	// PeerIdentity is one configured WireGuard peer's effective outer-control PSK
677	// and stable name/id (G4 multi-peer concentrator groundwork).
678	type PeerIdentity struct {
679		// PSK is this peer's effective outer-control PSK: for a single-peer config
680		// it is the top-level Config.PSK (the pre-G4 back-compat default, unaffected
681		// by any per-peer psk); for a multi-peer config it is the peer's own PSK
682		// field. validate() rejects a per-peer psk when exactly one peer is
683		// configured, so the single-peer-with-distinct-per-peer-psk shape is not
684		// loadable via config.Load; this field still defends the invariant
685		// defensively for any Config value built directly.
686		PSK Key
687		// Name is this peer's stable identifier: its configured Name when set,
688		// otherwise a fallback derived from its public key (the first 8 bytes,
689		// lowercase hex — the same short-key form uapiConfig uses to name a peer
690		// in error messages), so every peer has a non-empty, stable id even when
691		// name is omitted.
692		Name string
693	}
694	
695	// PeerIdentities returns, for each configured WireGuard peer in order, its
696	// effective PSK and stable name/id (G4). This is the SINGLE place the
697	// single-peer/multi-peer PSK back-compat decision is made: device.Up and the
698	// Bind consume PeerIdentities instead of each re-deriving the effective PSK
699	// from Config.PSK vs Peer.PSK. Order matches c.WireGuard.Peers.
700	func (c Config) PeerIdentities() []PeerIdentity {
701		peers := c.WireGuard.Peers
702		ids := make([]PeerIdentity, len(peers))
703		for i, p := range peers {
704			psk := p.PSK
705			if len(peers) == 1 {
706				// Single-peer back-compat: the top-level psk remains the effective
707				// PSK regardless of whether a per-peer psk is also set.
708				psk = c.PSK
709			}
710			name := p.Name
711			if name == "" {
712				pub := p.PublicKey.Bytes()
713				name = hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8])
714			}
715			ids[i] = PeerIdentity{PSK: psk, Name: name}
716		}
717		return ids
718	}

243	// up drives the tunnel bring-up over an already-created TUN and an injected resolver factory: it

248	// privileged tun.CreateTUN. The same path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI
249	// fields cfg carries (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).

285		// is what the UAPI render installs on the engine peer; a hostname that does not resolve in the
286		// boot window (or a resolver that fails to construct) leaves its spec EMPTY and the peer boots
287		// WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot). The re-resolution loop then completes the wiring on
288		// first success via the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70). Literal specs are seeded verbatim, so
289		// an all-literal peer boots byte-for-byte as before.

368	// applyMetricsLocked reconciles the running /metrics endpoint to listen: it starts the
369	// endpoint when one is desired and none runs, stops it when listen is empty, and rebinds
370	// (stop old, start new) when the address changed. It is idempotent for an unchanged
371	// address. The Source is reused across a rebind so its derived-throughput state is not
372	// reset. The caller MUST hold reloadMu (Up and Reload both do), which also serializes it
373	// against Close reading metricsSrv. On a NewServer/refuse error the previous server is
374	// left running untouched, so a bad reload never drops a working endpoint.
375	func (t *Tunnel) applyMetricsLocked(listen string) error {
376		if listen == t.metricsListen {
377			return nil
378		}
379		if listen == "" {
380			t.stopMetricsLocked()
381			return nil
382		}
383		srv, err := metrics.NewServer(listen, t.metricsSrc, t.log)
384		if err != nil {
385			return err
386		}
387		t.stopMetricsLocked()
388		srv.Start()
389		t.metricsSrv = srv
390		t.metricsListen = listen
391		return nil
392	}
393	
394	// stopMetricsLocked gracefully shuts the running endpoint down (if any) and clears the
395	// bookkeeping. Caller holds reloadMu.
396	func (t *Tunnel) stopMetricsLocked() {
397		if t.metricsSrv == nil {
398			return
399		}
400		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), metricsShutdownTimeout)
401		defer cancel()
402		if err := t.metricsSrv.Close(ctx); err != nil {
403			t.log.Warn("metrics endpoint shutdown error", "error", err.Error())
404		}
405		t.metricsSrv = nil
406		t.metricsListen = ""
407	}
408	
409	// Reload applies a reloaded configuration to the RUNNING tunnel by diffing its
410	// path set against the live one and adding/removing paths at runtime (T30), WITHOUT
411	// tearing the tunnel down: the WG session, the surviving paths, and in-flight
412	// resequencing are all preserved. cfg is assumed already validated (config.Load
413	// fails fast on a bad file, so a bad reload never reaches here and never disturbs
414	// the running tunnel). Only the path set is diffed — the crypto/amnezia/role fields
415	// are fixed for the life of the process and a reload does not re-key the engine.
416	//
417	// New paths are added BEFORE absent ones are removed, so a failed addition aborts
418	// with every existing path still in service. Diffing is by name: a path present in
419	// both sets is left untouched (its source/dest parameters are not re-read).
420	//
421	// A membership-only reload CANNOT apply every kind of change: a same-name path whose
422	// source/dest changed, a path REORDER (index 0 is the preferred primary, so a reorder
423	// is a priority change), and every non-path field (psk, wireguard, amnezia, role, log,
424	// metrics) are all out of scope for T30. Rather than silently diverge from the file,
425	// Reload logs an EXPLICIT WARNING per ignored change (reloadWarnings) so the operator
426	// knows exactly what was dropped; only the path-membership add/remove is applied.
427	func (t *Tunnel) Reload(cfg *config.Config) error {
428		t.reloadMu.Lock()
429		defer t.reloadMu.Unlock()
430	
431		for _, w := range reloadWarnings(t.cfg, cfg) {
432			t.log.Warn("reload: ignored config change (path-membership reload only)", "change", w)
433		}
434	
435		// Rebind the /metrics endpoint if its listen address changed (T23). This is one of
436		// the few non-path fields a reload DOES apply: the endpoint is external to the WG
437		// session and the Bind, so restarting it disturbs neither. applyMetricsLocked reuses
438		// the tunnel's stable Source (its derived-throughput state survives), and on a refuse
439		// (e.g. a newly non-loopback address) leaves the previous endpoint running and fails
440		// the reload — the running tunnel is never disturbed. Applied BEFORE the path diff so
441		// a metrics-refuse aborts before any membership change.
442		if cfg.Metrics.Listen != t.metricsListen {
443			if err := t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen); err != nil {
444				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload metrics endpoint: %w", err)
445			}
446			t.log.Info("reload: metrics endpoint rebound", "listen", cfg.Metrics.Listen)
447		}
448	
449		add, remove := diffPaths(t.bind.PathNames(), cfg.Paths)
450		for _, def := range add {
451			if err := t.bind.AddPath(def); err != nil {
452				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload add path %q: %w", def.Name, err)
453			}
454			t.log.Info("reload: path added", "path", def.Name)
455		}
456		for _, name := range remove {
457			if err := t.bind.RemovePath(name); err != nil {
458				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload remove path %q: %w", name, err)
459			}
460			t.log.Info("reload: path removed", "path", name)

2338	// source_addr was not assignable at Open) has no socket, reader, or scheduler entry:
2339	// removing it merely drops it from the durable membership + deferred set, so a reload
2340	// that DROPS a still-deferred path retires it cleanly.
2341	//
2342	// Since a tolerant Open leaves m.defs/m.probers (durable, full length) LONGER than
2343	// m.paths (bound only), the durable-membership splice is keyed by IDENTITY (name),
2344	// NOT by the m.paths index — indexing m.defs by the m.paths position would splice the
2345	// wrong entry once a deferred path precedes the removed one.
2346	func (m *Multipath) RemovePath(name string) error {
2347		m.mu.Lock()
2348		defer m.mu.Unlock()
2349		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
2350			return errClosed
2351		}
2352		if _, ok := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler); !ok {
2353			return errors.New("bind: scheduler does not support runtime path membership")
2354		}
2355		// Locate the path in the DURABLE membership by identity. m.defs (and each peer's
2356		// probers) are full-length (bound + deferred); m.shared is the bound subset and may be
2357		// shorter.
2358		defIdx := -1
2359		for i := range m.defs {
2360			if m.defs[i].Name == name {
2361				defIdx = i
2362				break
2363			}
2364		}
2365		if defIdx < 0 {
2366			return fmt.Errorf("bind: remove path %q: no such configured path", name)
2367		}
2368		// Is it a LIVE (bound) shared socket? If so it owns a socket + per-peer scheduler
2369		// entries to retire.
2370		sharedIdx := -1
2371		for i, sp := range m.shared {
2372			if sp.name == name {
2373				sharedIdx = i
2374				break
2375			}
2376		}
2377		if sharedIdx < 0 {
2378			// A DEFERRED path: no transport to tear down — just drop it from the durable
2379			// membership and the deferred set so it does not resurrect on the next Open.
2380			m.removeDurableLocked(defIdx, name)
2381			return nil
2382		}
2383		// Removing a bound path: refuse if it is the LAST live socket (that tears down the
2384		// virtual endpoint the engine holds). A deferred path carries no transport, so it
2385		// does not count toward "at least one live path must remain".
2386		if len(m.shared) == 1 {
2387			return fmt.Errorf("bind: refusing to remove path %q: at least one live path must remain", name)
2388		}
2389		sp := m.shared[sharedIdx]
2390		// FAN-OUT (single owner): drop this shared path's per-(peer,path) view from EVERY bound
2391		// peer — its scheduler entry and its peerPathState — so no peer schedules onto the
2392		// closing socket. Each peer's remaining paths are untouched (the splice is by identity).
2393		for _, p := range m.peers {
2394			if err := m.detachPeerPathBoundLocked(p, name); err != nil {
2395				return err
2396			}
2397		}
2398		m.shared = append(m.shared[:sharedIdx], m.shared[sharedIdx+1:]...)
2399		m.removeDurableLocked(defIdx, name)
2400		// Closing the socket unblocks and retires the path's reader; it is NOT waited on
2401		// here (it never touches the path slice, and any last in-flight frame it Observes
2402		// is delivered normally), so a removal never blocks the caller behind a read.
2403		return sp.conn.Close()
2404	}
2405	
2406	// removeDurableLocked drops the path named name from the durable membership: m.defs and
2407	// EVERY peer's probers at defIdx (each peer's probers is kept index-aligned with m.defs),
2408	// and the deferred set by name (a no-op when the path was bound rather than deferred).
2409	// Caller holds m.mu. Keying the durable splice on defIdx (a name lookup) rather than a
2410	// bound-path index is what keeps m.defs / each peer's probers correct once a tolerant Open
2411	// has made them longer than the bound path list — so a subsequent Close→Open rebuilds
2412	// exactly the surviving membership and neither resurrects the removed path nor loses a
2413	// deferred one.
2414	func (m *Multipath) removeDurableLocked(defIdx int, name string) {
2415		m.defs = append(m.defs[:defIdx], m.defs[defIdx+1:]...)
2416		for _, p := range m.peers {
2417			if p.probers != nil {
2418				p.probers = append(p.probers[:defIdx], p.probers[defIdx+1:]...)
2419			}
2420		}
2421		for i := range m.deferred {
2422			if m.deferred[i].def.Name == name {
2423				m.deferred = append(m.deferred[:i], m.deferred[i+1:]...)
2424				break
2425			}
2426		}
2427	}
2428	
2429	// PathNames returns the names of the DURABLE configured membership — every path the
2430	// bond is configured for, in priority order, INCLUDING a path that is currently
2431	// DEFERRED because its source_addr is not yet assignable (it is in m.defs but not
2432	// m.paths). The device's config reload diffs the desired path set against this to
2433	// decide what to add or remove; returning the deferred paths here is what keeps a
2434	// no-op reload a no-op — a still-configured deferred path is NOT seen as a new add
2435	// (which AddPath would then reject on the same EADDRNOTAVAIL bind), so the
2436	// SIGHUP-no-op invariant holds across the whole deferred window.
2437	func (m *Multipath) PathNames() []string {
2438		m.mu.Lock()
2439		defer m.mu.Unlock()
2440		names := make([]string, len(m.defs))
2441		for i, def := range m.defs {
2442			names[i] = def.Name
2443		}
2444		return names
2445	}
2446	
2447	// PathTraffic is a consistent per-path traffic+telemetry snapshot (T23): the OUTER-
2448	// wire byte counters fused with the path's telemetry quality Estimate and liveness
2449	// State (read verbatim from its Prober). It is the shape the metrics.Source adapter
2450	// maps into a metrics.PathSnapshot; the Bind reports raw cumulative byte counters and
2451	// the Estimate/State — it does NOT compute a rate here (the adapter derives throughput
2452	// from the byte-counter delta across scrapes). Estimate/State are the telemetry
2453	// zero-values on a bind without the probe transport (no prober).
2454	type PathTraffic struct {
2455		Name     string
2456		TxBytes  uint64
2457		RxBytes  uint64
2458		Estimate telemetry.Estimate
2459		State    telemetry.PathState
2460	}
2461

The gap is: (1) a public API to register additional peers, (2) device.Up wiring, (3) reporting per-peer virt endpoints. Let me read the peer-fanout tests, virtualEndpoint, newReceiveFunc, and how peers get registered/reported.

1618	// Send wraps each buffer in an outer DATA frame (fresh outer-seq + the chosen
1619	// path's id) and writes it to that path's remote. The egress path is chosen by
1620	// the injected scheduler (T15): the active-backup policy returns the preferred
1621	// primary while it is up and the failover backup otherwise. The scheduler
1622	// selects by path priority/liveness only; the Bind additionally requires THAT
1623	// ONE chosen path to have a known remote, failing the send otherwise rather
1624	// than silently dropping.
1625	//
1626	// Behavioural change from pre-T15: this is a deliberate NARROWING. The removed
1627	// pickPathLocked iterated the paths and skipped any healthy-but-remoteless one,
1628	// falling through to the next healthy path WITH a known remote, so a send failed
1629	// only when NO path had a remote. Now the scheduler owns selection and returns a
1630	// single index; the Bind does NOT fall through, because a Bind-level fall-through
1631	// would bypass the scheduler's hysteresis (failover/failback). The residual
1632	// window this opens: if the scheduler's chosen path is reported Up but its remote
1633	// is not yet learned — e.g. a concentrator/hub restart, or a T16 NAT-rebind
1634	// before the first inbound packet re-teaches the remote — the send fails until
1635	// that path's remote is learned, even if another path has a known remote.
1636	//
1637	// Critical-section discipline: path selection and framing run under m.mu (the
1638	// send Codec is shared and stateful — D5's single keystream requires sequential,
1639	// mutex-guarded Encode), but each datagram is encoded into its OWN fresh buffer
1640	// so it outlives the lock, and the WriteToUDPAddrPort syscalls run WITHOUT m.mu
1641	// held. The scheduler is internally synchronized and never calls back into the
1642	// Bind, so calling Pick under m.mu cannot deadlock and adds no receive-path
1643	// contention (the lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path is untouched). A receive
1644	// goroutine pinning the virtual endpoint therefore never blocks behind an
1645	// in-flight transmit syscall.
1646	func (m *Multipath) Send(bufs [][]byte, ep Endpoint) error {
1647		ue, ok := ep.(*udpEndpoint)
1648		if !ok {
1649			return conn.ErrWrongEndpointType

1651	
1652		// Classify the batch by inner WireGuard message type (defect D22): a handshake or
1653		// keepalive is passed to the scheduler as ClassControl so a pacing scheduler exempts
1654		// it from the per-path data token buckets and bulk overload cannot shed it and starve
1655		// rekey. The classifier is parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia profile, so it works
1656		// under advanced-security obfuscation (custom magic headers + handshake junk), not
1657		// only vanilla WireGuard. It reads only the (possibly junk-shifted) type word off the
1658		// lock. It is peer-agnostic (inner WireGuard type, not routing), so it runs before the
1659		// peer resolution below.
1660		class := m.classify.classifyBatch(bufs)

1662		m.mu.Lock()

1664		// DISTINCT virt, so this datagram egresses on THAT peer's outerSeq, scheduler,
1665		// sendCodec, fecSend, and per-(peer,path) set — never the embedded primary's by
1666		// promotion. An endpoint of the right type but unknown to the demux (no bound peer)

130	}
131	
132	// peerPathState is one peer's per-(peer,path) VIEW of a shared uplink: that peer's own
133	// decode Codec (each path receives on its own goroutine, so the Codec's scratch is never
134	// shared), the peer's own learned/configured return remote, the peer's own probe
135	// initiator for this path, and the peer's own per-path OUTER-wire byte counters. The
136	// remote is either configured (edge dest_addr / peer endpoint) or LEARNED from inbound
137	// traffic (concentrator) — but it lives strictly BELOW the engine's single virtual
138	// endpoint, so the engine never sees this per-(peer,path) bookkeeping churn.
139	//
140	// It EMBEDS its *sharedPathState so the socket identity (name/id/src/conn) is reached
141	// transparently, and back-references the owning peerState so a receive handler resolves
142	// exactly that peer's resequencer/reflector/FEC decoder.
143	type peerPathState struct {
144		*sharedPathState
145		peer  *peerState
146		codec *frame.Codec
147		// prober is this (peer,path)'s own probe initiator (nil when the bind runs without the
148		// probe transport). It is set at path creation and immutable for the path's life,
149		// so the Bind-owned receive goroutine reaches it via the peerPathState it already
150		// holds — NOT through a shared, dynamically-mutated probers slice — which is
151		// what keeps echo handling race-free while paths are added/removed at runtime (T30).
152		prober *telemetry.Prober
153	
154		// txBytes/rxBytes are cumulative OUTER-wire byte counters for this (peer,path), the
155		// per-path traffic accounting the /metrics exposition reports (T23). txBytes counts
156		// the DATA-frame wire bytes this path egresses on the Send hot path; rxBytes counts
157		// every outer datagram this path's readLoop receives (DATA, PROBE, and echo alike —
158		// the true received wire volume). They are atomics so the send/receive hot paths
159		// increment them WITHOUT taking m.mu (lock-free), and the scrape/snapshot path reads
160		// them with a plain atomic Load; a single writer per counter (Send holds m.mu while
161		// choosing the path, and each path has exactly one readLoop) means the Add is
162		// uncontended in the common case. "Bytes on Starlink vs 5G" — the data-thrift signal
163		// (requirement 2) — is exactly txBytes: when the weighted scheduler collapses to the
164		// primary under sub-capacity load, the backup path's Send count stays ~flat.
165		txBytes atomic.Uint64
166		rxBytes atomic.Uint64
167	
168		mu        sync.Mutex
169		remote    netip.AddrPort
170		hasRemote bool
171	}
172	
173	// peerState holds the per-PEER datapath state that was a process-global singleton before
174	// the multi-peer concentrator split: the SINGLE virtual endpoint the engine holds for this
175	// peer, the peer's send scheduler, its own outer-seq space, its shared send Codec, its
176	// probe reflector, its receive resequencer and FEC send/receive planes, its per-path probe
177	// initiators, and its per-(peer,path) views over the shared sockets. The single-peer
178	// edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split
179	// singleton — Multipath embeds it as the primary and the datapath reaches its fields through
180	// that embed); the concentrator constructs one peerState per bound peer. resequencer and
181	// fecRecv stay atomic.Pointer, and outerSeq atomic, so the lock-free receive/send fast paths
182	// read them WITHOUT m.mu.
183	type peerState struct {
184		// name is the peer id/name, the key under which Multipath.peersByName holds this peer.
185		// Empty on the single-peer edge/hub (there is only one peer to key).
186		name string
187	
188		// psk is THIS peer's effective pre-shared key: the sole seam from which every frame
189		// Codec this peer derives (its sendCodec and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via
190		// newCodec) and its probe Reflector authenticate. The single-peer edge/hub sets it to
191		// the one configured psk (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton);
192		// the concentrator sets a DIFFERENT psk per bound peer, so one peer's codec/reflector
193		// rejects another peer's frames (T84). NewCodec/NewReflector PSK-derivation itself is
194		// unchanged — only WHICH psk each peer feeds them differs.
195		psk config.Key
196	
197		// Immutable per-peer collaborators, built at construction and persisting across the
198		// Open→Close socket lifecycle (the concentrator pins virt's destination once for the
199		// process life, so virt in particular must NOT be recreated per Open).
200		virt      *udpEndpoint
201		scheduler sched.Scheduler
202		reflector *telemetry.Reflector
203		// newProber mints a prober for a path admitted to THIS peer at runtime (T30); nil
204		// disables runtime path addition for the peer (a bind without the probe transport).
205		newProber ProberFactory
206		// probers is this peer's BOOT-TIME per-path probe initiator set, in durable-membership
207		// (m.defs) order — bound AND deferred — shared with this peer's scheduler. At Open each
208		// bound entry is bound onto its peerPathState (pp.prober), and thereafter the hot paths
209		// reach a path's prober through the peerPathState — never by indexing this slice — so a
210		// runtime path add/remove cannot race echo handling. Nil when the peer runs without the
211		// probe transport; a nil here also gates the whole probe loop off.
212		probers []*telemetry.Prober
213	
214		// Per-Open state, (re)built by Open and cleared by Close.
215		sendCodec *frame.Codec
216		paths     []*peerPathState
217		outerSeq  atomic.Uint64
218		// resequencer is this peer's shared T18 receive resequencing buffer. Published
219		// atomically so the per-path readLoop goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu.
220		resequencer atomic.Pointer[reseq.Resequencer]
221		// FEC datapath (T24), per peer. Both published atomically like resequencer so the
222		// lock-free receive fast path Loads fecRecv, and the lazy re-instantiation on re-bind
223		// (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated, on a readLoop that must not take m.mu) can Store
224		// fecSend without racing Send's under-m.mu Load. The ENCODER inside fecSend is still
225		// mutated (Admit/Tick) only under m.mu — the atomic pointer governs publication of the
226		// fecSender object, not its encoder's single-writer discipline. Both nil when FEC is off.
227		fecSend atomic.Pointer[fecSender]
228		fecRecv atomic.Pointer[fecReceiver]
229	
230		// lifecycleMu serializes lazy (re)instantiation of the heavy trio (resequencer, fecRecv,
231		// fecSend) on a readLoop goroutine (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated) against teardown's
232		// clearing of that trio (teardownPeerLocked), so a teardown interleaving mid-instantiation
233		// can never leave a half-published plane (a fecRecv/fecSend without its resequencer) nor
234		// resurrect a plane on a torn-down peer that the next re-bind then reuses stale. It is a
235		// LEAF lock taken either ALONE (instantiation — which must never take m.mu, so Close's
236		// readersWG.Wait under m.mu cannot deadlock on it) or UNDER m.mu (teardown), giving the
237		// single fixed order m.mu -> lifecycleMu and no cycle (instantiation never reaches for m.mu).
238		lifecycleMu sync.Mutex
239	}
240	
241	// newPeerState builds the durable per-peer datapath state whose probe Reflector — and,
242	// through ps.psk, every frame Codec this peer later derives in Open/AddPath (its sendCodec
243	// and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via newCodec) — authenticate under THIS peer's
244	// psk. It is the seam that replaces the pre-split single-psk assumption (T84): the
245	// single-peer edge/hub mints exactly one (the primary) from the sole configured psk, while
246	// the concentrator mints one per bound peer from that peer's DIFFERENT effective psk, so a
247	// peerState built from a different psk gets its own codec/reflector and cross-psk frames are
248	// rejected. virt is created here (not per Open) because the concentrator pins its destination
249	// once for the process life. The Reflector draws its per-path challenges from crypto/rand,
250	// exactly as before. The per-Open fields (sendCodec, paths, resequencer, FEC planes) are
251	// left zero for Open to (re)build.
252	func newPeerState(name string, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, newProber ProberFactory, probers []*telemetry.Prober) *peerState {
253		return &peerState{
254			name:      name,
255			psk:       psk,
256			virt:      &udpEndpoint{},
257			scheduler: scheduler,
258			reflector: telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader),
259			newProber: newProber,
260			probers:   probers,
261		}
262	}
263	
264	// newCodec derives a fresh frame Codec bound to THIS peer's psk — the send Codec at Open and
265	// each per-(peer,path) receive Codec share the derivation but not the instance, since a Codec
266	// is not safe for concurrent use (each receive path decodes on its own goroutine). Deriving
267	// from ps.psk (not a Multipath-wide key) is what makes a path's receive Codec the codec of the
268	// peer the path is bound to (T84).
269	func (ps *peerState) newCodec() (*frame.Codec, error) {
270		return frame.NewCodec(ps.psk)
271	}
272	
273	// deferredPath is a configured path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr was not yet
274	// assignable at Open (net.ListenUDP -> EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the
275	// address, e.g. a 5G modem with no DHCP lease at boot). Rather than tear the whole
276	// bond down, Open records the path here instead of binding it: the tunnel comes up
277	// on the paths that DID bind, and this path stays DOWN — its prober, never fed an
278	// echo, reports StateDown, which the scheduler excludes from Pick, exactly as the
279	// runtime path-down model treats a live-but-silent path. It carries the boot-time
280	// prober so the T55 background reconcile that retries the bind reuses the SAME
281	// path-id stamp instead of minting a new one. prober is nil only on a bind without
282	// the probe transport, which never defers (see Open).
283	type deferredPath struct {
284		def    config.Path
285		prober *telemetry.Prober
286	}
287	
288	func (ps *peerPathState) setRemote(ap netip.AddrPort) {
289		ps.mu.Lock()
290		ps.remote, ps.hasRemote = ap, true
291		ps.mu.Unlock()
292	}
293	
294	func (ps *peerPathState) getRemote() (netip.AddrPort, bool) {
295		ps.mu.Lock()
296		defer ps.mu.Unlock()
297		return ps.remote, ps.hasRemote
298	}
299	
300	// Multipath is the P1 bonding conn.Bind: one UDP socket per configured path
301	// (bound to the path's source address), all fronted by a SINGLE stable virtual
302	// endpoint the engine holds per peer. Send wraps each opaque WireGuard datagram
303	// in an outer DATA frame (own outer-seq + path-id) and picks a path; one
304	// Bind-owned reader per path unwraps DATA frames into the shared resequencer, and
305	// a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc drains it and hands the inner WG datagram up
306	// under the shared virtual endpoint (the fan-in that lets paths be added/removed at
307	// runtime without the engine spawning receive goroutines — T30). It replaces
308	// bind.Passthrough behind the same conn.Bind seam (device wiring is unchanged).
309	//
310	// Lifecycle: the sockets live for the duration of an Open→Close span, NOT the
311	// Multipath's whole life. The amneziawg engine calls Close() before every Open()
312	// (device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked, and on IpcSet listen_port /
313	// route-change events) and cycles Close↔Open on Down/Up, so — exactly like
314	// conn.StdNetBind — Open creates the per-path sockets and Close tears them down
315	// AND clears the path state so the next Open rebuilds from scratch. The "closed"
316	// state is simply "no bound sockets" (len(paths)==0); there is no separate sticky
317	// flag that a later Open would have to reset.
318	//
319	// Path policy: an injected sched.Scheduler (T15) chooses the egress path per
320	// datagram. The P1 MVP wires an active-backup scheduler (all traffic on the
321	// preferred primary, transparent failover to a backup on a T13 path-DOWN signal
322	// with failback hysteresis); the weighted/FEC-aware policy (T21) is a different
323	// Scheduler swapped in here with no Bind change. No FEC/resequencing yet
324	// (P2/P3), but every DATA frame carries its outer-seq and path-id so T18/T24 can
325	// consume them.
326	// ProberFactory mints a *telemetry.Prober for a path admitted at runtime (T30),
327	// stamped with the given stable path-id. The device wires it capturing the SAME
328	// per-boot session id, prober config, clock, PSK, and logger the boot-time probers
329	// were built with, so a runtime path's liveness is measured identically to a
330	// boot-time path (its probes join the same session so the peer's reflector adopts
331	// them without a challenge reset). It is nil on a bind built without the probe
332	// transport (the T12 unit tests), which therefore cannot add paths at runtime.
333	type ProberFactory func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober
334	
335	type Multipath struct {
336		defs []config.Path
337	
338		// classify maps each outbound datagram to its pacer traffic class from the inner
339		// WireGuard message type, parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia obfuscation profile
340		// so a WireGuard control frame (handshake/keepalive) is recognised — and pacing-
341		// exempted — under advanced security too, not only in vanilla mode (defect D22). It
342		// is immutable after construction and holds no lock, so Send reads it off m.mu.
343		classify wgClassifier
344	
345		// deferredListen binds a reconciled deferred path's socket (T55 background
346		// reconcile). It is an injection seam: the default pins the source IP unless dev is
347		// set (net.ListenUDP / listenPath, matching AddPath's runtime bind — I5), and a test
348		// overrides it to drive the deferred→bound transition deterministically — a
349		// source_addr "becoming assignable" — without a real interface address having to
350		// appear on the host. dev is resolveForcedDeviceBind's decision for the deferred
351		// path's resolved BindMode, computed by reconcileDeferred before the call. Immutable

649	func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
650		m.mu.Lock()
651		defer m.mu.Unlock()
652		if len(m.paths) != 0 {
653			return nil, 0, conn.ErrBindAlreadyOpen
654		}
655	
656		// (Re)build the per-Open datapath planes — send Codec, receive resequencer, and FEC
657		// send/receive state — fresh for this bring-up, for EVERY bound peer (not just the
658		// primary via promotion). This keeps Open symmetric with closeSocketsLocked, which
659		// clears every peer's per-Open state: a concentrator peer bound before Open must get
660		// its OWN fresh planes on each Close→Open cycle, and one peer's (re)creation must never
661		// touch another peer's release point or FEC group state. On the single-peer edge/hub
662		// m.peers holds only the primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split rebuild.
663		// See openPeerDatapathLocked.
664		for _, p := range m.peers {
665			if err := m.openPeerDatapathLocked(p); err != nil {
666				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
667				return nil, 0, err
668			}
669		}
670	
671		// Resolve every path's interface up front and decide, per path, whether its
672		// socket may be device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard — survives a T16
673		// re-roam) or must pin the specific source IP (the pre-T16 behaviour, required
674		// when paths share an interface or the interface is multi-address, so distinct
675		// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port without an EADDRINUSE collision). See
676		// selectDeviceBinds.
677		srcs := make([]netip.Addr, len(m.defs))
678		modes := make([]config.BindMode, len(m.defs))
679		for i := range m.defs {
680			srcs[i] = m.defs[i].SourceAddr
681			modes[i] = m.defs[i].Bind
682		}
683		bindDevs := planPathBinds(srcs, modes)
684	
685		// A path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr is merely NOT-YET-ASSIGNABLE at boot
686		// (EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the address — a 5G modem without a DHCP
687		// lease, Starlink mid-obstruction) is DEFERRED rather than treated as fatal, so
688		// the bond comes up on the paths that DO bind (G2/W1, approach A). Tolerating it
689		// requires the runtime path-down machinery: the deferred path's prober stays
690		// StateDown so the scheduler excludes it, and a DynamicScheduler whose membership
691		// can be reconciled to only the bound paths. Absent the probe transport (the T12
692		// no-prober unit binds) or a dynamic scheduler there is no such Down model, so —
693		// exactly as AddPath refuses a runtime add without probers — every bind error
694		// stays fatal there. A MALFORMED source_addr never reaches here (config.validate
695		// rejects it at load), and any OTHER bind error (EADDRINUSE, permission) is fatal.
696		dyn, dynOK := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
697		tolerateDefer := m.probers != nil && dynOK
698		m.deferred = nil
699	
700		actualPort := port
701		firstBound := true
702		// boundProbers is index-aligned with m.paths (the paths that actually bound), so
703		// the scheduler reconcile below and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping stay aligned
704		// even when a middle path deferred. nil in the no-prober case (nothing to reconcile).
705		var boundProbers []*telemetry.Prober
706		for i := range m.defs {
707			def := m.defs[i]
708			// Device-bind this path when selectDeviceBinds proved it safe (so a
709			// mid-session source-address change / T16 re-roam does not break the socket),
710			// otherwise pin the specific source IP. See selectDeviceBinds / listenPath.
711			c, err := listenPath(def.SourceAddr, port, bindDevs[i])
712			if err != nil {
713				if tolerateDefer && errors.Is(err, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL) {
714					// Defer this path: record its def + boot prober (kept Down) for the T55
715					// background reconcile to retry, and leave the bond to come up on the rest.
716					// Mirrors AddPath's rollback discipline — a failed path never disturbs the
717					// tunnel — at the boot boundary.
718					m.deferred = append(m.deferred, deferredPath{def: def, prober: m.probers[i]})
719					continue
720				}
721				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
722				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: open path %q on %s: %w", def.Name, def.SourceAddr, err)
723			}
724			// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort: the kernel caps at net.core.rmem_max and
725			// SetReadBuffer does not require privilege, so a returned error is rare;
726			// treat it as non-fatal rather than refusing to bind in a restricted env.
727			_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
728	
729			// The path binds to the primary peer here; its receive codec is that peer's codec
730			// (reached through the embedded primary, as the datapath reaches m.sendCodec).
731			codec, err := m.newCodec()
732			if err != nil {
733				_ = c.Close()
734				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
735				return nil, 0, err
736			}
737	
738			shared := &sharedPathState{name: def.Name, id: uint8(i), src: def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
739			ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec}
740			if m.probers != nil {
741				ps.prober = m.probers[i]
742				// Reconcile the DATA-frame path-id to the prober's IMMUTABLE stamp rather than
743				// to the slice index i: after a runtime RemovePath the survivor keeps its
744				// original (higher) stamp, so index-based numbering would renumber a live path
745				// AND diverge its DATA id from its PROBE stamp. Taking id from the prober keeps
746				// DATA and PROBE agreeing on the wire and a survivor's id stable across a reopen.
747				shared.id = ps.prober.PathID()
748				boundProbers = append(boundProbers, ps.prober)
749			}
750			switch {
751			case def.DestAddr.IsValid():
752				ps.setRemote(def.DestAddr)
753			case m.hasDefaultRemote:
754				ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
755			}
756			m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
757			m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
758			// Publish the primary's view for the receive demux (T88). On the single-peer edge/hub
759			// this is the ONLY view, which handleInbound reads as the byte-identical fast path; a
760			// concentrator peer bound later appends its own view to the same socket.
761			shared.addViewLocked(ps)
762			if firstBound {
763				actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
764				firstBound = false
765			}
766		}
767	
768		// Hard guard (a): a bond with NO transport is impossible. If every configured path
769		// deferred, tolerance must NOT degrade to a zero-path bind — fail fatally, exactly
770		// as the pre-tolerance Open did when the sole path could not bind.
771		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
772			_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
773			return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: no configured path could bind its source address (all %d deferred as not-yet-assignable)", len(m.deferred))
774		}
775	
776		// nextPathID is a MONOTONIC HIGH-WATER carried ACROSS Open spans, never decreased.
777		// Close does not reset it, and here it is only ever RAISED to at least max(existing
778		// path stamp)+1. Resetting it to len(m.paths) (the pre-fix behaviour) re-minted an id
779		// a survivor still held once a RemovePath had opened a gap in the stamp space below
780		// that survivor's stamp: the next runtime AddPath then collided with the live path at
781		// an identical (PathID, SessionID), and because the peer's Reflector keys anti-replay
782		// AND the session challenge PER PathID, the strict-monotonic replay filter dropped one
783		// of the two independent ProbeSeq streams -> probe loss / false-DOWN. Deriving the
784		// high-water from the (prober-stamped) ps.id covers both the probe-transport case and
785		// the T12 no-prober case (where ps.id == i, so the high-water is len(m.paths)).
786		m.openPort = port
787		for _, ps := range m.paths {
788			if uint16(ps.id)+1 > m.nextPathID {
789				m.nextPathID = uint16(ps.id) + 1
790			}
791		}
792		// A DEFERRED path's boot prober already OWNS its path-id stamp; raise the
793		// high-water past it too, so a runtime AddPath arriving while the path is still
794		// deferred cannot mint that reserved id and collide with it once T55 binds it.
795		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
796			if dp.prober != nil {
797				if uint16(dp.prober.PathID())+1 > m.nextPathID {
798					m.nextPathID = uint16(dp.prober.PathID()) + 1
799				}
800			}
801		}
802	
803		// Reconcile the scheduler's membership with the path slice just rebuilt from
804		// m.defs. A runtime AddPath/RemovePath (T30) keeps m.defs, m.probers, AND the
805		// live scheduler in lockstep during an Open span; re-pinning the scheduler's
806		// health list to the BOUND probers HERE — index-aligned with m.paths, since each
807		// bound path appended its ps.prober to boundProbers in order above — is the single
808		// reconciliation point that makes that runtime membership survive this
809		// Close→Open cycle without a scheduler/path desync (no frozen zombie health
810		// entry, no resurrected removed path). A DEFERRED path (not-yet-assignable
811		// source_addr) is deliberately EXCLUDED here: its prober is absent from the
812		// scheduler, so Pick can never select it, and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping
813		// stays index-aligned with the bound-only path slice. Only meaningful with the
814		// probe transport: a bind without probers cannot change membership at runtime
815		// (AddPath is refused) and never defers, so its scheduler is left exactly as built.
816		if m.probers != nil && dynOK {
817			health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(boundProbers))
818			for i, pr := range boundProbers {
819				health[i] = pr
820			}
821			if err := dyn.SetPaths(health); err != nil {
822				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
823				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: reconcile scheduler on open: %w", err)
824			}
825		}
826	
827		// Stand up the receive fan-in: one Bind-owned reader per path feeding the shared
828		// resequencer, and a single engine-facing drainer. Both channels are recreated
829		// per Open so a Close→Open cycle starts clean.
830		m.deliverSignal = make(chan struct{}, 1)
831		m.recvClosed = make(chan struct{})
832		for _, ps := range m.paths {
833			m.readersWG.Add(1)
834			go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)
835		}
836		// Deadline-tick goroutine for FEC group close (T24): it flushes a partial group's
837		// parity on time under low load so a group is never stranded waiting for the size
838		// threshold. Tracked by readersWG (like the readers) so Close waits for it; it
839		// exits on recvClosed. It TryLocks m.mu, so it never blocks Close's readersWG.Wait
840		// held under m.mu.
841		if m.fecSend.Load() != nil {
842			m.readersWG.Add(1)
843			go m.fecTickLoop(m.fecCfg.Deadline, m.recvClosed)
844		}
845		return []ReceiveFunc{m.newReceiveFunc(m.deliverSignal, m.recvClosed)}, actualPort, nil
846	}
847	
848	// openPeerDatapathLocked (re)builds ONE peer's per-Open datapath planes: its send Codec
849	// (derived from THIS peer's psk), its receive resequencer, and — when FEC is configured —
850	// its FEC send/receive planes. Each is fresh per Open so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect)
851	// re-pins the peer's release point and re-anchors its FEC grouping to this bring-up rather
852	// than a stale prior span. It writes ONLY the given peer's fields (from the peer's own psk
853	// and the bind-wide fecCfg/adaptiveCfg), so one peer's (re)creation never touches another
854	// peer's resequencer or FEC group state — the per-peer lifecycle boundary that keeps a
855	// reconnect on one peer from disturbing another's. Caller holds m.mu; on error the caller
856	// unwinds the whole Open via closeSocketsLocked (which clears every peer's per-Open state),
857	// so a partial build here is cleaned up.
858	func (m *Multipath) openPeerDatapathLocked(ps *peerState) error {

1001	// Owning the readers in the Bind (rather than returning one ReceiveFunc per path to
1002	// the engine, as T12 did) is what makes a runtime path add/remove possible without
1003	// disturbing the engine: the engine builds its receive goroutines ONCE from Open's
1004	// return, so a path added later gets a reader HERE while the engine's fixed receive
1005	// set — and thus the WG session — sees no churn. The goroutine exits when its socket
1006	// is closed (RemovePath drains one path; Close drains all).
1007	func (m *Multipath) readLoop(ps *peerPathState, deliver chan<- struct{}) {
1008		defer m.readersWG.Done()
1009		readBuf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
1010		for {
1011			n, srcAP, err := ps.conn.ReadFromUDPAddrPort(readBuf)
1012			if err != nil {

2099				}
2100			}
2101		}
2102		m.shared = nil
2103		m.deferred = nil

897	// uapiConfig renders cfg into the newline-delimited UAPI set string the engine's
898	// IpcSet consumes. Keys are lowercase hex (UAPI's on-the-wire encoding), NOT the
899	// base64 form the TOML carries. Amnezia obfuscation keys are emitted only when the
900	// block is configured; an all-zero Amnezia block leaves the engine in plain
901	// WireGuard mode. The same amnezia parameters are applied on BOTH roles (edge and
902	// concentrator) as defense-in-depth — they must match end to end for the handshake
903	// to succeed (config validation makes each end specify a complete profile, D1).
904	func uapiConfig(cfg *config.Config, bootEndpoints []bootEndpoint) (string, error) {
905		var b strings.Builder
906	
907		priv := cfg.WireGuard.PrivateKey.Bytes()
908		fmt.Fprintf(&b, "private_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(priv[:]))
909		if cfg.Role == config.RoleConcentrator {
910			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "listen_port=%d\n", cfg.WireGuard.ListenPort)
911		}
912		writeAmnezia(&b, cfg.Amnezia)
913	
914		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
915			pub := peer.PublicKey.Bytes()
916			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "public_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(pub[:]))
917			// The engine peer's endpoint is built ONLY from a resolved flattened entry (Q30): the
918			// boot endpoint is the head of the peer's seeded failoverSpecs — a literal's fixed address
919			// for an all-literal peer (byte-for-byte the pre-G5 render, Endpoints[0]), or a hostname's
920			// boot-resolved head. When nothing resolved (a hostname peer whose name did not resolve in
921			// the boot window, or a resolver-less concentrator peer with no endpoint at all) the boot
922			// endpoint is INVALID and no endpoint line is emitted — the peer boots endpoint-less
923			// (tolerant boot), and for a hostname peer the re-resolution loop installs it on first
924			// success (R70). Switching to a standby endpoint on hub loss is the failover controller's
925			// job, not this initial render.
926			if ep := bootEndpoints[i]; ep.valid {
927				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "endpoint=%s\n", ep.ap.String())
928				// A keepalive keeps the edge->concentrator session warm and lets the
929				// concentrator relearn the edge endpoint after a NAT rebind; only the
930				// initiating (edge) side sets it.
931				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "persistent_keepalive_interval=%d\n", keepaliveSeconds)
932			}
933			if len(peer.AllowedIPs) == 0 {
934				return "", fmt.Errorf("peer %d (%s): at least one allowed_ip is required", i, hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8]))
935			}
936			for _, cidr := range peer.AllowedIPs {
937				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "allowed_ip=%s\n", cidr)
938			}
939		}
940		return b.String(), nil
941	}
942	
943	// keepaliveSeconds is the edge's persistent-keepalive interval.
944	const keepaliveSeconds = 25

946	// writeAmnezia emits the nine amneziawg obfuscation UAPI keys, but only when the
947	// block is configured. When amnezia is unused, wanbond leaves the engine's
948	// PROCESS-GLOBAL message-type state untouched (see the amneziaGuard invariant).
949	// Config validation guarantees a configured block is complete and self-consistent
950	// (D1), and applyDefaults has already filled the standard magic headers (1..4)
951	// when they were omitted, so the emitted set never carries an h*=0 sentinel.
952	func writeAmnezia(b *strings.Builder, a config.Amnezia) {
953		if !a.Configured() {
954			return
955		}

958	}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"sync"
5		"time"
6	
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
8	)
9	
10	// emitProbes performs one probe cadence step: for every currently-open path it
11	// emits one authenticated PROBE frame (IsEcho=false) to that path's learned/
12	// configured remote, then Ticks that path's Prober so liveness advances against
13	// the injected clock. A path without a known remote yet is still Ticked (so a
14	// silent path is detected Down) but nothing is sent — there is nowhere to send.
15	//
16	// Concurrency mirrors Send: the path/prober set is snapshotted under m.mu, then
17	// released before any socket I/O, so emission neither holds the lock across a
18	// syscall nor blocks the lock-free receive fast path. A concurrent Close closes
19	// the sockets out from under the snapshot; the resulting write error is benign
20	// (teardown) and dropped. It is a no-op when the bind has no probers or is closed.
21	func (m *Multipath) emitProbes() {
22		m.mu.Lock()
23		if len(m.paths) == 0 || m.probers == nil {
24			m.mu.Unlock()
25			return
26		}
27		type target struct {
28			ps *peerPathState
29			pr *telemetry.Prober
30		}
31		targets := make([]target, 0, len(m.paths))
32		for _, ps := range m.paths {
33			if ps.prober == nil {
34				continue
35			}
36			targets = append(targets, target{ps: ps, pr: ps.prober})
37		}
38		m.mu.Unlock()
39	
40		for _, t := range targets {
41			if remote, ok := t.ps.getRemote(); ok {
42				if raw, err := t.pr.SendProbe(); err == nil {
43					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe; this races no in-flight Send.
44					_, _ = t.ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(raw, remote)
45				}
46			}
47			t.pr.Tick()
48		}
49		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18): recompute the scheduler's active egress path
50		// each probe cadence so a liveness DOWN switches egress even when no application
51		// Send is driving Pick. This is the timer-driven companion to the receive-tick
52		// nudge (see tickLivenessFromReceive) — under CPU starvation this loop's ticker
53		// lags, which is why the receive-tick path carries the guarantee, but when the
54		// loop does run it keeps the selection fresh independent of egress traffic.
55		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
56	}
57	
58	// StartProbeLoop launches the probe cadence goroutine: it calls emitProbes every
59	// interval until the returned stop function is invoked. The caller (device.Up)
60	// starts it AFTER the engine has opened the bind and stops it BEFORE Close, so the
61	// loop only runs while the sockets exist (emitProbes is a safe no-op if it races a
62	// closed bind). The returned stopper is idempotent.
63	//
64	// The cadence uses a wall-clock ticker because it is production timing glue, not
65	// liveness logic: every liveness decision the loop drives runs through the
66	// injected telemetry.Clock the Probers hold (SendProbe stamps it, Tick reads it),
67	// so tests drive emitProbes directly against a fake clock and never start this
68	// goroutine. It is a no-op (returning a no-op stopper) when the bind has no
69	// probers or interval <= 0.
70	func (m *Multipath) StartProbeLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
71		if m.probers == nil || interval <= 0 {
72			return func() {}
73		}
74		// Arm the receive-path liveness sweep at the same cadence (D15): a receiver may
75		// now advance liveness when the timer goroutine below is starved under load.
76		m.sweepIntervalNanos.Store(int64(interval))
77		done := make(chan struct{})
78		go func() {
79			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
80			defer ticker.Stop()
81			for {
82				select {
83				case <-done:
84					return
85				case <-ticker.C:
86					m.emitProbes()
87				}
88			}
89		}()
90		var once sync.Once
91		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
92	}

1045	func (m *Multipath) tickLivenessFromReceive(now time.Time) {
1046		interval := m.sweepIntervalNanos.Load()
1047		if interval == 0 {
1048			return // no probe transport / loop not started: nothing to Tick
1049		}
1050		n := now.UnixNano()
1051		last := m.lastSweepNanos.Load()
1052		if n-last < interval {
1053			return // throttled: this interval's sweep was already taken
1054		}
1055		if !m.lastSweepNanos.CompareAndSwap(last, n) {
1056			return // another receive goroutine won this interval's sweep
1057		}
1058		// Snapshot the prober set under m.mu (a runtime AddPath/RemovePath mutates it),
1059		// then Tick OUTSIDE the lock so a transition's log write never runs under m.mu.
1060		// TryLock, NOT Lock: Close holds m.mu WHILE it waits on readersWG for the readers to
1061		// exit, so a reader that BLOCKED here on m.mu would deadlock that shutdown. The sweep
1062		// is opportunistic and throttled, so when the lock is contended (a concurrent
1063		// Close/AddPath/RemovePath/Send) simply skipping this interval is harmless — the
1064		// probe-loop ticker and the next receive still advance liveness. This preserves
1065		// Close's invariant that a reader never blocks on m.mu. The lock, when taken, is held
1066		// at most once per interval (~5/s) for a bounded snapshot, so it adds negligible
1067		// contention to Send and does not disturb the lock-free receive fast path.
1068		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
1069			return
1070		}
1071		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, 0, len(m.paths))
1072		for _, ps := range m.paths {
1073			if ps.prober != nil {
1074				probers = append(probers, ps.prober)
1075			}
1076		}
1077		m.mu.Unlock()
1078		for _, pr := range probers {
1079			pr.Tick()
1080		}
1081		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18, the repeated-flap wedge). The active-backup
1082		// selection is otherwise recomputed ONLY inside the scheduler's Pick, which the
1083		// Bind calls ONLY from Send — so failover is pull-based: it happens only when the
1084		// engine hands down an egress datagram. When the ACTIVE path dies during an egress
1085		// LULL that is fatal: a repeated-flap kill landing on the just-restored primary
1086		// before the saturating flow re-fills it (the failback and the next kill overlap)
1087		// leaves both TCP directions stalled on the now-dead path, so NO Send occurs, so
1088		// Pick is never called, so egress is never switched to the healthy backup — the
1089		// bond wedges on the dead path until the 25s WG keepalive finally drives a Send.
1090		// The receive tick is the starvation-robust signal that ALREADY detected the DOWN
1091		// (it just Ticked the path down above); recomputing the scheduler from it makes the
1092		// switch EAGER — bounded by the detection window (~DownAfter + one interval) rather
1093		// than by the next application Send. Pick recomputes purely against current liveness
1094		// and the clock and the failback dwell is time-based, so a more frequent recompute
1095		// can only make a genuine transition land sooner — never a premature/false failover,
1096		// and never a shortened anti-thrash hysteresis. It takes ONLY the scheduler's own
1097		// lock (never m.mu), so it adds no receive-path m.mu contention and cannot invert
1098		// the Send-path m.mu→scheduler lock order.
1099		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
1100	}
1101	
1102	// nudgeSchedulerActive forces the scheduler to recompute its active egress path
1103	// against current liveness, independent of any application Send. It is the eager-
1104	// failover companion to the Send-driven Pick: driven from the liveness-detection
1105	// paths (the receive tick and the probe loop) it switches egress to a healthy backup
1106	// the moment the active path is detected DOWN, so a dead active path with stalled
1107	// egress does not wedge the bond until the next Send (defect D18). The returned index
1108	// is intentionally discarded — only the recompute (and its logged transition) is
1109	// wanted here; Send remains the sole reader of the selection for actual routing. Pick
1110	// is internally synchronized and never calls back into the Bind, so this is safe to
1111	// call from a receive goroutine or the probe loop without m.mu.
1112	//
1113	// It calls Recompute, NOT Pick: the nudge wants only the liveness-driven active-set
1114	// recompute (and its logged transition), and a weighted/aggregating scheduler's Pick
1115	// is STATEFUL — a spurious Pick here would consume a distribution slot and skew the
1116	// per-path weight split. Recompute is the non-consuming half that refreshes the
1117	// eligible/active set without touching distribution/pacing/load state, so the T40
1118	// eager-failover guarantee holds for BOTH the active-backup and the weighted policy
1119	// (defect D18).
1120	func (m *Multipath) nudgeSchedulerActive() {
1121		m.scheduler.Recompute()
1122	}

1444	func (m *Multipath) teardownPeerLocked(p *peerState) bool {
1445		if p == m.peerState {
1446			return false // the primary (edge/hub) is torn down only by Close, never by session loss
1447		}
1448		if m.peerIsLiveLocked(p) {
1449			return false // a live (Up) peer is never torn down, whatever other peers' churn
1450		}
1451		// Clear the heavy trio under lifecycleMu so a concurrent ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated on a
1452		// readLoop (which also holds lifecycleMu across its whole build-and-publish) cannot interleave:
1453		// it either runs wholly before this clear (and is then wholly undone here) or wholly after
1454		// (and rebuilds all three cleanly). This closes the resurrection/half-published hole where a
1455		// teardown landing mid-instantiation left a fecRecv/fecSend without its resequencer, which the
1456		// next re-bind then reused stale. lifecycleMu is a leaf lock (see its field doc); we hold m.mu
1457		// here, giving the fixed order m.mu -> lifecycleMu with no cycle. The unbind runs after the
1458		// clear (and outside lifecycleMu): it is its own lock-free CAS loop and needs no ordering here.
1459		p.lifecycleMu.Lock()
1460		p.resequencer.Store(nil)
1461		p.fecRecv.Store(nil)
1462		p.fecSend.Store(nil)
1463		p.lifecycleMu.Unlock()
1464		m.unbindPeerSources(p)
1465		return true
1466	}

1468	// TearDownPeer frees the heavy per-peer state of the named configured peer once its WireGuard
1469	// session / liveness is gone — the device wires this from its per-peer session events (Q26). It
1470	// is a no-op returning false when the peer is unknown, is the embedded primary, or is still
1471	// LIVE (a live peer is never torn down); it returns true when the peer's resequencer ring and
1472	// FEC buffers were freed and its source bindings released. A torn-down configured peer
1473	// re-instantiates cleanly on its next authenticated PROBE (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated).
1474	func (m *Multipath) TearDownPeer(name string) bool {
1475		m.mu.Lock()
1476		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1477		p, ok := m.peersByName[name]
1478		if !ok {

1480		}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"

14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
15		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
16		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
17	)
18	
19	// fakeClock is a hand-advanced telemetry.Clock. The probe-transport tests drive
20	// emitProbes / handleInbound synchronously on a single goroutine (no probe-loop
21	// goroutine, no receive goroutines), so the clock needs no internal
22	// synchronization and liveness transitions are deterministic under -race.
23	type fakeClock struct{ now time.Time }
24	
25	func newFakeClock() *fakeClock { return &fakeClock{now: time.Unix(1_700_000_000, 0)} }
26	
27	func (c *fakeClock) Now() time.Time          { return c.now }
28	func (c *fakeClock) advance(d time.Duration) { c.now = c.now.Add(d) }
29	
30	// probeLiveness are the detection thresholds these tests use: short enough to
31	// keep the fake-clock arithmetic terse, with the same shape as production
32	// (DownAfter spans several intervals, UpAfterSuccesses > 1 for hysteresis).
33	const (
34		testProbeInterval  = 100 * time.Millisecond
35		testProbeDownAfter = 300 * time.Millisecond
36		testProbeUpSucc    = 3
37		testProbeRTT       = 10 * time.Millisecond
38		// testProbeSessionID is the fixed per-boot probe session id these bind tests
39		// stamp their Probers with (T38/D12); any consistent value works because each
40		// Prober handles only echoes of its own probes.
41		testProbeSessionID uint64 = 0xABCDEF0123456789
42	)
43	
44	// newProbingMultipath builds a Multipath wired with one live *telemetry.Prober
45	// per path and a real active-backup scheduler over those SAME probers, plus the
46	// injected fake clock. FailbackAfter is set far beyond any test horizon so
47	// failback never interferes with a failover assertion.
48	func newProbingMultipath(t testing.TB, paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, clk telemetry.Clock) (*Multipath, []*telemetry.Prober, sched.Scheduler) {
49		t.Helper()
50		lg, err := log.New("error", io.Discard)
51		if err != nil {
52			t.Fatalf("build logger: %v", err)
53		}
54		cfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
55			LossWindow: 0,
56			Liveness:   telemetry.LivenessConfig{DownAfter: testProbeDownAfter, UpAfterSuccesses: testProbeUpSucc},
57		}
58		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
59			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, testProbeSessionID, psk, cfg, clk, lg)
60		}
61		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(paths))
62		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(paths))
63		for i := range paths {
64			probers[i] = newProber(paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
65			health[i] = probers[i]
66		}
67		scheduler, err := sched.NewActiveBackup(health, sched.Config{FailbackAfter: time.Hour}, clk, lg)
68		if err != nil {
69			t.Fatalf("build scheduler: %v", err)
70		}
71		m, err := NewMultipath(paths, psk, scheduler, probers, newProber, nil, nil, config.Amnezia{})
72		if err != nil {
73			t.Fatalf("NewMultipath: %v", err)
74		}
75		return m, probers, scheduler
76	}
77	
78	// rawPeer is a loopback UDP socket standing in for the remote (concentrator)
79	// end: the edge's per-path socket sends probes here, and the test reflects them.
80	func rawPeer(t testing.TB) (*net.UDPConn, netip.AddrPort) {
81		t.Helper()
82		c, err := net.ListenUDP("udp", &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.ParseIP("127.0.0.1")})
83		if err != nil {
84			t.Fatalf("listen raw peer: %v", err)
85		}
86		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = c.Close() })
87		return c, c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).AddrPort()
88	}
89	
90	// readProbe reads one datagram off a raw peer socket and decodes it as a probe.
91	func readProbe(t testing.TB, peer *net.UDPConn, codec *frame.Codec) frame.Probe {
92		t.Helper()
93		if err := peer.SetReadDeadline(time.Now().Add(2 * time.Second)); err != nil {
94			t.Fatalf("set read deadline: %v", err)
95		}
96		buf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
97		n, err := peer.Read(buf)
98		if err != nil {
99			t.Fatalf("read probe: %v", err)
100		}

`internal/wireaudit` and `TestP5DPI`/`TestWireFormatAudit`). Overhead is small
and fixed: **DATA ≈ 17 bytes**, **PARITY ≈ 18 bytes** of header on top of the
nonce; the daemon subtracts this from the TUN MTU so there is no fragmentation.

### The multipath Bind — `internal/bind`

The heart of wanbond: the `conn.Bind` implementation the engine drives. It:

- **presents one stable virtual endpoint per peer** to the engine while privately
  fanning out across the real per-path sockets beneath it (design rule **A1**, see
  [p0-findings.md §3](p0-findings.md)). The engine must never see per-packet
  endpoint churn, so every receive returns the *same* `*udpEndpoint`; the learned
  destination is held in an `atomic.Pointer` because the Bind writes it under its
  mutex while the engine reads it locklessly.
- **learns edge endpoints dynamically** — the concentrator needs no edge endpoint
  config; it discovers each path's (possibly NAT'd) source from inbound traffic,
  enabling real CGNAT traversal.
- owns the **per-path UDP sockets**, byte counters, the send-path FEC `Admit`, the
  adaptive-FEC tick loop, and the wiring that hands frames to the scheduler and
  the resequencer.
- classifies each outbound batch by inner WireGuard message type (parameterized by
  the configured AmneziaWG obfuscation profile — custom `h1`–`h4` magic headers
  and `s1`/`s2` junk prefixes) so control frames can be treated specially by the
  pacer.
- **tolerates a not-yet-assignable `source_addr` at startup** (`Open()`). A path
  whose *well-formed* `source_addr` no interface holds yet — a mobile edge booting
  before its 5G modem has a DHCP lease, Starlink mid-obstruction — makes
  `net.ListenUDP` return `EADDRNOTAVAIL`. Rather than tear the whole bond down,
  `Open()` brings the tunnel up on the paths that **do** bind and *defers* the
  unbindable ones: a deferred path is recorded (with its boot prober) and left

Realize the concentrator's per-peer datapath wiring on top of the T83-T91
peerState/demux split. device.Up now builds one prober set + scheduler +
prober factory per configured peer, each keyed on that peer's effective PSK
from config.PeerIdentities (R72/T84), and registers each additional peer with
the Bind before dev.Up drives Open.

Bind changes:
- AddConcentratorPeer registers an additional bound peer (its own psk,
  scheduler, boot probers, factory, and stable virtual endpoint) before Open.
- Open builds a per-(peer,path) view of every bound socket for EVERY peer (its
  own psk-derived codec + per-peer prober) and reconciles EACH peer's scheduler
  from its own bound probers. The primary (peers[0]) path is byte-identical to
  the pre-split single-peer path, so single-peer wiring is unchanged.
- The probe/liveness loop (emitProbes, tickLivenessFromReceive,
  nudgeSchedulerActive) drives every bound peer's probers/scheduler so a
  concentrator peer's liveness feeds its OWN scheduler.
- BoundPeerNames / PeerVirtEndpoints observability accessors.

The receive demux (source->peer binding on authenticated PROBE), per-peer virt
report to the engine (newReceiveFunc), and the runtime AddPath/RemovePath
fan-out were already multi-peer aware; this change makes device.Up actually
construct N peers so they are exercised.

device.Up keys the primary on PeerIdentities[0].PSK (== top-level psk for a
single-peer config, byte-identical to pre-G4). uapiConfig already renders all
peers, so the single-peer golden output is untouched. startHubFailover stays
edge-only and unchanged.

Tests: bind-package white-box (two peerStates each with own
scheduler/probers/virt; runtime AddPath fan-out keyed on each peer's OWN psk
with mutation-sensitive assertion; RemovePath teardown; registration refused
after Open) and device-level (2-peer concentrator up() -> two peerStates with
distinct virt; single-peer -> exactly one). Mutation-verified: a runtime prober
keyed on the wrong peer's psk fails the per-peer keying assertion.

Gate: go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... &&
go test ./... all green; gofmt clean.
